title: Patrick D Nielsen -Services Hiring Manager Interview | Technical Managing Editor
meeting_title: Patrick D Nielsen -Services Hiring Manager Interview | Technical Managing Editor

transcript.text: Well, hello, Andi.
How are you?
Good.
How are you?
Terrific.
Happy Friday to you.
Good to see you and meet you.
Happy Friday.
Where are you based?
I'm in the Salt Lake City area, so I'm up in the mountains here.
Lovely.
Are you a senior?
Are you there for summer activities?
What's your...
I am here, actually.
I moved here about 10 years ago from Texas, and me and my wife are out here in a little town called Morgan, just about 20 miles outside of Salt Lake.
Have you ever been out to this area?
I have, yeah.
My family used to have a temperature in Deer Valley.
Oh, that is lovely out there, right by Park City.
Yeah, we go up there on the weekends, and just there's a big thing that goes on in the summertime where they have estate sales, and you go in and find all these treasures.
That's so fun.
It is.
It is.
Amazing.
Yeah, it really is.
So where are you based out of?
I'm in Rhode Island.
I saw that on the LinkedIn, so that's where you're, okay.
Yeah, yeah, on the East Coast.
So it is, we have a heat advisory today, so.
Uh-oh.
It's a warm one.
We've had a lot of that here.
It's so dry.
We've had some wildfires lately, so things are.
Oh, wow.
Very, yeah, be very careful.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Well, thank you for scheduling time, especially on a Friday.
Absolutely.

I'll give you a little bit of background on myself, GrowthX, in the context of this role, and then we can talk about you, and I'll try and leave about 10 minutes at the end, 5 to 10 minutes, for you to ask me some questions.
Absolutely.
Absolutely.
Perfect.
Perfect.
So I am the customer operations lead at GrowthX, which basically means I manage kind of like the process and aspect.
All the aspects that go into delivering the content for clients.

So sort of thinking about from the product, which we have an internal platform that leverages AI to build workflows that generate content automatically, and in some cases automatically publish to their CMS.
And we have our teams work in pods, so as we're delivering content to clients, we have account directors, technical managing editors, which I think this role wears kind of two hats.

So thinking about project management, really, in terms of deliverables for the client, making sure we're on time, making sure that nothing's broken, and a little bit of an editorial layer on the content before it gets sent to the client.
But also, as project manager, making sure that the client is updated and
And we're kind of, we can shift strategy based on their feedback, or we can kind of shift the deliverable based on their feedback.
So the account director would work with this role to help set strategy.

And I mean, I think like depending on the personas, or less sort of client facing work, but a little bit of sort of technical chops, because we want somebody who can sort of identify problems in the workflows, help improve them, and then that project management piece.
So that's a little bit about what we're looking for, and what the team looks like.

And as I mentioned, GrowthX really is leveraging AI to produce content at scale for clients that are looking both for, to improve their SEO performance, and also we're starting to do a lot more work in...
At least, like, thought leadership work around geo, like, generative engine optimization is what we're doing at.
Very cool.
optimizing for, like, AIs and LLMs.
So, yeah, that's a little bit about the role and kind of the role that I play.
So you'd be reporting into me and also kind of dotted line to the director team that you're working with.
Fantastic.
Fantastic.
And I can see there's a lot there, Andi, that I can help the media with.
I know you've been there for a few months.
It's like a new team.
But what I've done over the past few years is immerse myself in AI and have used it to really help teams get the message out and a lot more power and a lot more clarity.
And I was reading an article or a statement from Marcel the other night as I was researching this.
And he made a comment that I thought was so...
so...
Which is that so...
want want So And you.

And thought about that and I said, you know, it's exactly right, because when you're doing prompt engineering and you're wanting to get the best results, you have to place yourself in that the material and content should be already there.
And you want to be able to direct it and drive it to a final message or a final result that the customer really needs.

And when I was talking to Maralia the other day, we were talking about teamwork and how you need to be part of this team and be flexible, be able to pivot when maybe a certain context isn't going the right way or certain results not exactly perfect, show that empathy for the customer.
I really feel that these are the kinds of things I can jump in with you and help immediately.
Yeah, great.
Well, talk to me a little bit about your experience.
Like, give me sort of...
The elevator pitch on your background and maybe what you've been doing more recently.
Sure, sure.
In the past five years, Andi, I've been looking at or working with Microsoft over the past five years with different contracts and different vendors.
And the last couple of years, purely AI with Shipley and with the Microsoft Ivis project.
And that was a virtual reality headset for the of Defense.
Recently, though, I dove deeply in the AI with the last year with Shipley, with different customers, all the way from WPS Global to Lego to Target.
And we dove into an AI called Responsive.
I don't know if you've heard of that platform.
No.
But Responsive is incredible.
It has three AI pieces in it.
And what it allowed to do is dive into these enormous proposals and these enormous amounts of questions and funnel in tens of thousands of pieces of content quickly to answer questions quickly.

And the amazing thing, Andi, is that it came up with a 50% to 53% correct answer ratio, so that my team could dive in and hone in on the answers that were already there, but then free up time to create answers that necessarily weren't there.
I see that AI can do that kind of thing.
And what I really mastered was prompt engineering.
I think a lot of folks that get into AI immediately forget the context, the tone, and what you're actually asking to produce.
A lot of folks, I think, don't do that the best way.
And I had a chance to really work on that.
And I love working with the engineering thinking behind it to get the best results.
Yeah, I'm just looking up responsive.
So it looks like it's a little bit of a proposal repository, and it's...
It worked with a lot of massive content lines.
So I had a lot of chance to become a knowledge manager as well with some of these pieces.
It was wonderful.
But the actual content strategy before that was on the high-end technical part.
But it lended itself well to customers like Lego, for example, or like Target, for example.
Because the customer in mind there is the shopper.
And you want to be able to get the best results that way.
So that is kind of me in a nutshell.
I'm an AI, I'm a lover of the AI prompt engineering tactic, as well as how content can best serve the customer.
There we go.
Awesome.
Well, can you tell me, talk me through one of these projects with responsive kind of, and what role you played and how you leveraged the AI and then what the end result was?
Absolutely.
So I think the best example was WPS Global.
They are the ones that had a proposal that had 3,000 questions.
So when we looked at that, we thought, thought, uh-oh.
How are going to manage this?
Because we had a turnaround time of about a week, five working days, really.
So what we did was, before that one happened, I had worked on a few others, so I was really immersed in responsive.
They have three AIs.
One of them is called Ask, the other one is called AI Assist, and the other one is AI Draft.

And what we would do, and what I did, is I took this enormous proposal, and instead of feeling overwhelmed with it, I dove into AI Assist to see how it could piece together the questions from the content library.
Once it was in responsive, then we went into AI Draft to actually answer the questions, and then craft them to how, what needed to happen to win that proposal.
Can you talk to me a little bit about, like, the inputs, for example, into Assist?
So how you piece together the pieces, and, like, what prompts you use?
Absolutely.
When you would go into a question, and let's say, for example,
For that the AI didn't hit the white glove sites or the white list of sites.
I would go in and immediately prompt engineer what the question was, what I wanted the answer to be based on the SOP and based on the actual proposal.
And then when the answer came, I would do my editing and polishing, make sure it was nice and that it read perfectly.
And then that would go to the marketing team to see if it was on target or correct.
So it was a lot of flexibility, a lot of pivoting with different teams.
But the AI allowed for the prompt to look at the tone of the voice, the context of what was needed, if it was technical, if it was more sales pitched.
And I think it worked out beautifully that way.
The deal was won and they just loved how the AI worked and how fast it worked.
I think it really lends itself well to what GrowthX is doing because I had a chance to see how massive content could be.
it's worth So gonna say, All right.
How fast AI could go in there and make the human element that much more productive.
Yeah.
Oh, so what did you use to prompt it on tone, text, and context?
I would prompt engineer like I would do a chat GPT prompt.
I would go in there and I would tell them that, give me an answer based on a Walmart associate or a WGPS associate or a Lego associate.
Go in there and use the context of the customer.
I would tell it what I was.
I would give it examples of I have been in the field for 10 years.
I had my education based out of Brooklyn.
And here's what I want the tone to sound like.
I've been a professional writer for 10 years.
And my audience is a group of customers that want to buy this product and convince me why it's the best.
All of that, I think, impromptu.
Andi Bailey, is mandatory to get the best results.
Yeah.
Does that help?
Yeah.

I was curious, like, so we use projects, we use projects in Claude, perplexity, and so there's a lot of opportunity to add context, just like if, I mean, I'm guessing with 3,000 questions, they probably give you some context on what they were looking for, too.
They did.
And of course, you had the proposal itself that gave you.
So you'd always go to your blueprint, but then you'd draft and even massage from there.
Yeah.
Cool.
Well, so obviously the other piece of this is really working with the client side.
So talk to me about your role in managing kind of, were you just writing the RFPs, were you interacting with the client, or if you have other experience in kind of client management from that.
Absolutely.
And the client management for me was key.
Because we had to lead the customer by the hand and assure them that, for one, everything was on target for deadline.
Number two, if there were questions that we weren't able to answer, how do we get those answers and how do we provide them?
But really nurturing them along the way.
I love to be in front of the customer.
And I think that listening is one of the key components of customer success.
You want the customer to know that what they say and what they need is invaluable.
And if you don't have something that they need or want, and you can feel that, you have to assure them that you'll find it, that you'll get it by this time and date, and then deliver.
And I think communication that way and being transparent and showing that you care is critical.
And I enjoy the fact that customers, in my experience and what I've been able to do, I have this ability to gain trust, to show people, hey, I've...
I really do care about this.
I want you to succeed.
I want to succeed with you.
I think that's invaluable.
And it worked to my benefit.
It always has.
So in terms of client management, so was that like pre-RFP approval, pre-when you won, or were you managing the project through like post-winning the project?
I managed the project to submission.
So I would work with other writers or other editors, and I would work with marketing folks that would actually do the submission.
And that, Andi, it's a good question, because that came from the Microsoft side.
I was on the vendor side.
And so that helped me nurture the relationship of Microsoft as well.
I had to be the one that the Microsoft personnel and the marketing team needed to trust as well.
Because at the end, what was critical was getting the submission done, making sure it was perfect.
And you had to kind of manage those teams.
And I used Microsoft Project, of course.
To keep things organized.
Ticketing system, JIRA, for example, Confluence, use a lot of that to keep things.
ADO, DevOps, I used that in the previous one with high-tech Cedrals that was for the IBIS project.
So I have the tools, and the tools are fantastic, but without that ability to manage time, to respect time, and to nurture the customers, showing them, hey, we got this.
We got it.
The other tools don't really matter, right?
Yeah.
How do you think about prioritization, especially were you ever working on multiple proposals at a time?
Yes.
Yes.
And I think it's a, I'm a checklist guy.
That's how I kind of manage the teams I've ever led.
And that's how I manage my work, Andi.
I believe in checklists.
So when I, when we did scrum meetings every week, or we had a two-week sprint, the number one thing was, for my team or for myself, well, well,
What's the most important thing this week?
What's the most important thing this day?
What's the most high priority that my manager said has to happen that the customer needs?
And I would keep those with me every day, and I would keep those with me every week.

And if I kept on track with what was needed and what the deadlines were, and what needed to speak to me, and who I needed to speak to, I've always been successful managing by using my checklist, by keeping things prioritized based on leadership.
Based on who's the most important customer today, never pushing aside the others, but just making sure that we're on the same page moving forward week to week, sprint to sprint.
Yeah.
Talk to me about working in ambiguity, instances of ambiguity.
We're a startup.
There's a lot of changes that happen.
And like this week, we've sort of had three different things that happen where somebody has to drop something and pick something else up.
Right away.
And also just like not a lot of clarity, you know, you're going to have to like make snap decisions and also like operate with in areas of ambiguity and be scrappy.
So can you give me examples of when you've done that?
I think the best example was with Lego and also with Target, because in the back of my head, it was a simple thing.
But those instructions were not always that way.
And that was the case also with Vantage that I worked with.
It was another proprietary company that worked with rehab and folks getting out of rehab.
That's probably the best example, because the application allowed someone who came out of that harsh environment to plug into a treatment team.
Well, there's a huge umbrella of what that can mean.
So for me, Andy, when ambiguity takes place, I like to.
Brainstorm with the team and throw out ideas to get to a point of clarity, but you have to be able to look at the big picture and say, okay, what could this be?
Could it be this?
How can I get to point A to point B?
I think you have to answer and ask questions, number one.
You have to be able to pivot if things change and if you're not on the right track.
And number three, listen.
I think ambiguity can be cleared up by listening.
And a lot of folks, I think listening can become a problem.
And over the years, I found that's one of the most key things.
If you don't understand something, listen carefully, ask questions about it, and then put ideas out and see what can work.
Yeah, yeah.
Okay.
Those are all the questions I had for you.
And I do have to hop two minutes early just because I have another client call that I have to prep for a little bit.
That's So,
Um, so what questions do have for me?
I do have some questions, Andi.
When I was talking to Marilee the other day, she was mentioning that some of the folks in this role haven't done as well as you've got, as you've expected.
Yeah.
So that's my question.
I want to be able to come in immediately, Andi, and make a difference and succeed and drive home things within 30, 60 days immediately.
What does that look like?
And how can I make that something you don't worry about anymore?

Yeah, so I think, um, the, the things to think about there are, um, again, like, the people that have succeeded have taken, like, taken what they saw, created something, a process for nothing, figured it out, we don't have a lot of documentation.
Um, and there aren't a lot of people to help, like, you just kind of have to pick up whatever you can find and, and stitch it all together.
Um, we're starting to get,
Better at that, but I think just like our onboarding is getting better, but we don't have a lot that's like, this is what you do in every instance.
Our platform, our internal platform is brand new, and so you have to be willing to ask the engineering team very specifically for help.
And just generally, like, be willing to reach out to teams where you need help, raise your hand when you need help, and also, like, do whatever you can to get something across the line.
So, like, I think there's not a lot of support if you don't know where you're going.
I was going to ask about that.

Yeah, so you have to be willing to, like, I mean, we're, like, we just don't have the engineering resources to say this is a project, this is the marketing person, this is the engineering person, and this is, so, like, there's a team of three, but, you know, you have to be willing to push and be scrappy on your own.
You have to be, and I came from a startup environment when the doc.
Calm revolution took place, and I was with a company called Mission Critical that went public, but I remember the excitement and electricity of the startup environment.
How have you best managed being in this team right now in just a short period of time?
What's some challenges you've had, and how can I help you reduce your stress?
Yeah, so I've worked in startups before, even at Uber, which was a 20,000-person company when I joined.
I was working in an incubator within Uber, so it really is just like being willing to make a decision and live with the consequences.
Like, sometimes you make the wrong decision, sometimes you're moving too fast, and so, like, the less that I need to manage somebody to get something done, the better.
Okay, I like that very much.
I know that you have to have a lot of flexibility in the startup, and you have to be a self-starter and manager.
Yeah, I've done that for many years.
I enjoy that.
But my goal, of course, is to make your life a lot easier so that we can be a stronger team.
What are some things you would want to see me do that first month?
I mean, I think you would need to be comfortable running a workflow and knowing kind of the components that how to generate an artifact that would make like a good input.

And then, you know, identifying the issues and problems with the outputs, because essentially right now this role is a little bit of our like QA and UX feedback internally as you're generating content externally.
Very good.
Very good.
So the questions need to be asked immediately and get, you know, I understand completely.
Workflows are something that I want to be able to immediately help you with and guide.
I think the last question I had was just kind of based on this conversation with Marilia.
What are some things that have happened with this role that I can help eliminate that have not succeeded?
I mean, it's just self-starters.
Some people just are not, you know, they need the management.
And we don't have enough, like, I'm essentially sort of managing 18 people right now, directly and indirectly.
So it's, you know, it's just the being able to work independently and figure out what needs to be done without a lot of help.
And knowing how to reach out to, like, cross-functionally when you need that help.
Okay, fantastic.
Well, Andi, I don't want to take up more of your time if you've got to run.
So are there any concerns that you have at all?
I feel very good about this role.
I really do.
Yeah, it was great to chat with you, Pat.
I'm sorry I was a little late, but I appreciate you letting me go a little early.
Oh, sure, sure.
We're making it work.
Yeah.
Great.
Well, thank you so much.
Enjoy your Friday, and have a good day.
Okay, you too.
Talk to you soon.
All right.
Thanks, Pat.
Bye.
Bye-bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Conduct an interview with Patrick D Nielsen for the Technical Managing Editor position at GrowthX.](https://fathom.video/share/Axv2oTq9zLDcJfWTDiZ34KPAZyJc_Ti5?tab=summary&timestamp=848.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Pat has extensive experience in AI, prompt engineering, and content strategy, particularly with Microsoft and various clients.](https://fathom.video/share/Axv2oTq9zLDcJfWTDiZ34KPAZyJc_Ti5?tab=summary&timestamp=1205.0)
  - [GrowthX is seeking a self-starter who can manage projects, improve workflows, and provide editorial oversight while working independently.](https://fathom.video/share/Axv2oTq9zLDcJfWTDiZ34KPAZyJc_Ti5?tab=summary&timestamp=1195.0)
  - [The role requires adaptability, strong client management skills, and the ability to work effectively in a fast-paced startup environment.](https://fathom.video/share/Axv2oTq9zLDcJfWTDiZ34KPAZyJc_Ti5?tab=summary&timestamp=1598.0)

## Topics

### GrowthX Role Overview

  - [Position: Technical Managing Editor with dual focus on project management and editorial oversight](https://fathom.video/share/Axv2oTq9zLDcJfWTDiZ34KPAZyJc_Ti5?tab=summary&timestamp=950.0)
  - [Responsibilities:](https://fathom.video/share/Axv2oTq9zLDcJfWTDiZ34KPAZyJc_Ti5?tab=summary&timestamp=950.0)
      - [Manage deliverables and timelines for clients](https://fathom.video/share/Axv2oTq9zLDcJfWTDiZ34KPAZyJc_Ti5?tab=summary&timestamp=997.0)
      - [Provide editorial layer on content before client submission](https://fathom.video/share/Axv2oTq9zLDcJfWTDiZ34KPAZyJc_Ti5?tab=summary&timestamp=997.0)
      - [Identify and improve workflow issues](https://fathom.video/share/Axv2oTq9zLDcJfWTDiZ34KPAZyJc_Ti5?tab=summary&timestamp=1036.0)
      - [Act as a bridge between account directors and strategy implementation](https://fathom.video/share/Axv2oTq9zLDcJfWTDiZ34KPAZyJc_Ti5?tab=summary&timestamp=1030.0)
  - [Company leverages AI for content production at scale, focusing on SEO and generative AI optimization](https://fathom.video/share/Axv2oTq9zLDcJfWTDiZ34KPAZyJc_Ti5?tab=summary&timestamp=1063.0)

### Pat's Experience and Skills

  - [5 years working with Microsoft on various contracts, including AI projects](https://fathom.video/share/Axv2oTq9zLDcJfWTDiZ34KPAZyJc_Ti5?tab=summary&timestamp=1206.0)
  - [Recent focus on AI with Shipley, working with clients like WPS Global, Lego, and Target](https://fathom.video/share/Axv2oTq9zLDcJfWTDiZ34KPAZyJc_Ti5?tab=summary&timestamp=1225.0)
  - [Expertise in prompt engineering and AI platforms (e.g., Responsive AI)](https://fathom.video/share/Axv2oTq9zLDcJfWTDiZ34KPAZyJc_Ti5?tab=summary&timestamp=1242.0)
  - [Strong background in content strategy and customer-focused writing](https://fathom.video/share/Axv2oTq9zLDcJfWTDiZ34KPAZyJc_Ti5?tab=summary&timestamp=1347.0)
  - [Experience in managing large-scale proposals (e.g., 3,000-question RFP for WPS Global)](https://fathom.video/share/Axv2oTq9zLDcJfWTDiZ34KPAZyJc_Ti5?tab=summary&timestamp=1373.0)
  - [Proficient in project management tools: Microsoft Project, JIRA, Confluence, ADO, DevOps](https://fathom.video/share/Axv2oTq9zLDcJfWTDiZ34KPAZyJc_Ti5?tab=summary&timestamp=1748.0)

### Client Management and Project Handling

  - [Emphasis on nurturing client relationships and building trust](https://fathom.video/share/Axv2oTq9zLDcJfWTDiZ34KPAZyJc_Ti5?tab=summary&timestamp=1620.0)
  - [Experience managing projects through submission, coordinating with writers, editors, and marketing teams](https://fathom.video/share/Axv2oTq9zLDcJfWTDiZ34KPAZyJc_Ti5?tab=summary&timestamp=1703.0)
  - [Uses checklists and prioritization techniques to manage multiple projects](https://fathom.video/share/Axv2oTq9zLDcJfWTDiZ34KPAZyJc_Ti5?tab=summary&timestamp=1792.0)
  - [Advocates for clear communication, transparency, and meeting deadlines](https://fathom.video/share/Axv2oTq9zLDcJfWTDiZ34KPAZyJc_Ti5?tab=summary&timestamp=1663.0)

### Startup Environment and Expectations

  - [GrowthX requires adaptability and the ability to work with ambiguity](https://fathom.video/share/Axv2oTq9zLDcJfWTDiZ34KPAZyJc_Ti5?tab=summary&timestamp=1598.0)
  - [Limited documentation and support; need for self-starters who can create processes](https://fathom.video/share/Axv2oTq9zLDcJfWTDiZ34KPAZyJc_Ti5?tab=summary&timestamp=2029.0)
  - [Expectation to reach out cross-functionally when needed and push projects forward independently](https://fathom.video/share/Axv2oTq9zLDcJfWTDiZ34KPAZyJc_Ti5?tab=summary&timestamp=2062.0)
  - [Importance of making decisions quickly and living with consequences in a fast-paced environment](https://fathom.video/share/Axv2oTq9zLDcJfWTDiZ34KPAZyJc_Ti5?tab=summary&timestamp=2141.0)

## Next Steps

  - [Pat to demonstrate ability to run workflows and generate quality content inputs within the first month](https://fathom.video/share/Axv2oTq9zLDcJfWTDiZ34KPAZyJc_Ti5?tab=summary&timestamp=1308.0)
  - [Focus on identifying and addressing issues in content outputs](https://fathom.video/share/Axv2oTq9zLDcJfWTDiZ34KPAZyJc_Ti5?tab=summary&timestamp=1308.0)
  - [Provide QA and UX feedback internally while generating content externally](https://fathom.video/share/Axv2oTq9zLDcJfWTDiZ34KPAZyJc_Ti5?tab=summary&timestamp=1308.0)
  - [Pat to showcase self-starting capabilities and cross-functional collaboration skills](https://fathom.video/share/Axv2oTq9zLDcJfWTDiZ34KPAZyJc_Ti5?tab=summary&timestamp=2062.0)
